skill.name: generate-sparkle-appcast
skill.description: Generate a Sparkle appcast for Mos from the latest notarized build zip, release notes, git history, and an Ed25519 signing key.
skill.summary: Creates signed Sparkle appcast and release note files for Mos releases.
skill.author: Caldis

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, environment, and sensitive-key patterns. Review confirms the script is a plausible release automation tool, but it handles a Sparkle private signing key and writes temporary key material, so publication should include a clear security warning.

medium_findings.title: Private Sparkle Signing Key Handling
medium_findings.description: The script requires a Sparkle private key, reads it into a shell variable, passes the base64 seed to Python as a process argument, and writes DER and PEM key material in a temporary directory before signing the zip. This is legitimate for appcast generation, but it exposes sensitive signing material to local process inspection and temporary storage risks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The private key file requirement and conversion flow are explicit in both the documentation and script. The use is release-signing related, so the issue is credential handling risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Release Automation Executes Local Commands
medium_findings.description: The script runs python3, git, openssl, wc, date, cp, mkdir, mktemp, and rm to inspect the build zip, derive release notes, sign the archive, and write appcast files. Arguments are mostly quoted and Python subprocess calls do not use a shell, but running this skill still grants broad local command and filesystem effects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is clearly present and central to the workflow. I did not find shell=True or unquoted user input execution, which lowers confidence that this is an injection vulnerability.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Publication Targets
low_findings.description: The GitHub and Mos URLs are used to build appcast download links, release notes links, and XML namespace metadata. I did not find evidence that the script performs outbound network requests or exfiltrates data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in generated appcast content and configurable release note paths. No curl, wget, fetch, or other outbound request pattern was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many SKILL.md findings are Markdown command examples and file paths inside backticks. They document expected usage and outputs rather than executable code in the skill file itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed SKILL.md locations are prose, bullets, and inline code spans. They do instruct the user to run the release script, but they are not Ruby or shell backtick execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings point at XML description text and appcast fields, while the actual signature flow uses Ed25519 through OpenSSL. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or another weak algorithm in the reviewed script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines include description and release note XML content, not cryptographic algorithm selection. The signing commands operate on an Ed25519 key imported through OpenSSL.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Key Material In Temporary Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The private seed is decoded into DER form, converted to PEM, and used to create a signature from files under a temporary directory. Cleanup is registered, but users should run this only on trusted machines and protect the key file.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The temporary key paths and OpenSSL import/sign commands are explicit. The mktemp directory and trap reduce risk, but the pattern still handles sensitive material on disk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Release Files Written Into Build And Docs
dangerous_patterns.description: The script creates release note HTML files and appcast XML under build and docs directories. This is expected behavior, but users should review generated files before publishing them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file write and copy targets are visible and match the documented purpose. The risk is accidental publication of generated content, not hidden persistence.

content.value_statement: Publishing Mos releases requires a valid Sparkle appcast, signed zip metadata, and hosted release notes. This skill automates those files from the latest build zip, git history, and Sparkle signing key.
actual_capabilities: Finds the newest Mos release zip in the build directory.
Parses version, beta flag, date, and build number from the zip filename.
Reads Info.plist from the app bundle inside the zip.
Generates Chinese and English release notes from recent git commits.
Creates a Sparkle appcast.xml with download URL, size, version data, and Ed25519 signature.
Copies appcast and release note files into docs for publication.
limitations: Designed specifically for the Caldis Mos repository layout and filename pattern.
Requires local git, python3, openssl, build, docs, and Sparkle key files.
Does not upload releases or verify that GitHub assets already exist.
Handles private signing material, so it should run only in trusted environments.

use_cases.title: Ship a Stable Mos Release
use_cases.description: Generate the appcast and hosted release notes after creating a notarized stable zip.

use_cases.title: Publish a Beta Update Channel
use_cases.description: Create appcast metadata that marks a beta build with the Sparkle beta channel.

use_cases.title: Prepare Local Release Artifacts
use_cases.description: Produce files for review before committing docs updates or publishing a GitHub release.

prompt_templates.title: Generate From Last Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the generate-sparkle-appcast skill to create the Mos appcast using --since <previous-release-commit>. Show me the generated file paths and any errors.

prompt_templates.title: Check Release Inputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Before running the appcast script, verify that build, docs, the latest Mos zip, git, python3, openssl, and sparkle_private_key.txt are available.

prompt_templates.title: Review Generated Notes
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate the appcast, then summarize the Chinese and English release notes that were written for this tag.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Release Metadata
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the appcast workflow for the selected commit range, then verify the tag, download URL, version fields, file length, release notes links, and Ed25519 signature fields.

output_examples.input: Generate an appcast for the latest stable zip since the previous release commit.
output_examples.output: The workflow selects the newest Mos zip, creates localized release notes, signs the archive, writes build/appcast.xml, and copies publishing files into docs.
The generated appcast includes beta channel metadata, release note links, version fields, download size, and Sparkle signature information.

output: Selected zip and release tag summary
Generated appcast path and docs copy path
Chinese and English release note file locations

best_practices: Run the script only on a trusted release machine with restricted access to the signing key.
Review generated release notes and appcast metadata before committing docs changes.
Re-run the script whenever the zip is rebuilt, repacked, or re-signed.
anti_patterns: Do not run the script with an untrusted Sparkle private key file.
Do not publish the generated appcast before confirming the GitHub release tag and asset name.
Do not use this workflow for repositories that do not match the Mos build and docs layout.

faq.question: What does this skill generate?
faq.answer: It generates Sparkle appcast XML and Chinese and English release note files for Mos releases.

faq.question: Does it upload files to GitHub?
faq.answer: No. It writes local files and builds URLs that must match the GitHub release asset.

faq.question: Why does it need a private key?
faq.answer: Sparkle requires an Ed25519 signature for the release archive so clients can verify updates.

faq.question: Can it generate beta appcasts?
faq.answer: Yes. A beta filename causes the generated appcast to include beta channel metadata.

faq.question: Can I provide custom release notes?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the documented release note file environment variables to point at existing HTML files.

faq.question: Is this safe to run in any workspace?
faq.answer: No. It executes local commands and handles signing keys, so use a trusted release environment.
